All I've done in this revision is to refactor it such that, rather than get the class's internal namespace more than once, (once with `dir()`, and then iterating over the MRO for each name), it relies on only one loop over the MRO, with a `set` to keep track of names we've already seen (because they were overwritten in the subclass). This is basically what `dir()` was doing for us in the first place, (excluding classes with exotic metaclasses, which would cause problems anyway).

I started looking through the MRO, and each of its classes' `__dict__`s, in the first place, to avoid having to guess which class attribute values are proper functions (which act as instance methods), and which are instances of `classmethod` and `staticmethod`. (For that matter, this should now exclude properties as well, which makes sense.) Because rather, these are implemented in the class `__dict__` as non-callable descriptors, (which wrap functions) – and so the `not callable(attr_value)` [line](https://github.com/spulec/freezegun/pull/88/files#diff-80ef255f3bb85cfd652668d719317c10R240) can now exclude these without further inspection. So, freezegun will only wrap undecorated functions, (and callable objects, unless we start checking with `inspect.isfunction`).

In the changes that I'll be submitting next I changed the `create_topic` method to be more "robust" and don't rely on the SimpleClient API or on `auto_create_topic=true` to be able to create a topic.

The method tries to use the API, if possible (Kafka > 0.10.1), to create a topic but, if not, falls back to using `kafka.admin.TopicCommand` to ensure the topic is created successfully.

This command takes numeric command line arguments for the `--partitions` and `--replication-factor` options and this is what cause me to hit an exception at this point of the code.

I could've just passed them as strings to `kafka_run_class_args` instead, but I found this was more elegant and could help avoid further issues later.

One thing I remember about `pip` is that it hides the output of `setup.py` , so end-users will not know their cached wheels lack C modules...

It would be nice if we could make `python setup.py bdist_wheel` **fail** unless PURE_PYTHON is set, in which case
1. pip will print the failure message to the user
2. pip will not cache a C-module-less wheel in ~/.cache
3. pip will fall back to running `python setup.py install` directly

1. changing `rm -r kolibri/dist/exercise_perseus_renderer` to `rm -r kolibri/dist/` to remove all these files

Sticking to PEP8 around naming test functions is bad idea as the functions names are used in different context.
The most important thing is that you know what the tests are about when you read the function names.
I like bdd specification style of test (wiki)[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Behavior-driven_development#Specialized_tooling_support], (ScalaTest as example)[http://www.scalatest.org], where you write a spec in plain English, if you know any good way of doing such thing in pytest please share.

In py.test I tend to follow this convention:

test_<object_of_test>__<what function is being tested> 

But I'm not sure consistent around that (I've forgotten to put double underscore to separate object from a feature that is being tested), I'm fine to adopt some good convention.
